text: The surgeon observes that the colon is frequently attached to the anterior abdominal wall or to the sides of the pelvis, indicating adhesions likely due to previous episodes of diverticulitis.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon, detaching it from the abdominal wall due to occlusion in the pelvic region.

The surgeon continues dissection of the colon, starting laterally to identify the pedicle. Lighted stents placed by urology are visible, aiding in the procedure. The mesentery appears thickened, which is a common observation during this stage.
The surgeon retracts the heavy colon upwards towards the anterior abdominal wall to locate the inferior mesenteric pedicle.
The surgeon attempts to locate the mesorectum by dissecting distally, then proceeds to bring the dissection more proximally along the colon.
The surgeon uses a lighted stent as a visual guide to identify the location of the left ureters during the procedure.
Dr. Crean assists at the bedside, using suction to help identify and clear the surgical planes during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses scissors and minimal electrocautery to carefully dissect and identify the normal anatomical plane within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses COT insufflation to aid in separating anatomical planes during the dissection process.
The surgeon continues the dissection, carefully removing fat surrounding the superior hemorrhoidal vein and artery, aiming to separate these structures for individual handling.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the area where the left ureter closely approaches the vessel, noting significant inflammation in this region. The background shows the absence of lighted stents, which have been lifted.

The surgeon attempts to locate the ureter in the surgical field, ensuring its identification before proceeding with the dissection of the vascular pedicle, which may involve the inferior mesenteric vein and artery or the superior hemorrhoidal vessels.
The surgeon selects a point at the proximal rectum and distal sigmoid colon, preferring to extend further down onto the rectum to ensure complete removal of the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to clear the mesorectum, achieving effective hemostasis. The mesorectum is visibly separated from surrounding tissues, highlighting the precision of the vessel sealer compared to staplers.
The surgeon carefully cleans the mesorectum, ensuring it is circumferentially cleared of surrounding tissue.
The surgeon cleans the distal colon and then takes the mesentery at the proximal point of the descending colon, preparing the site for the creation of a colorectal anastomosis.
The surgeon straightens the rectum and introduces dilators to evaluate the appropriate size of the end-to-end anastomosis (EA) stapler for the procedure.

The surgeon decides on a 29 EA stapler and creates a colotomy to facilitate passing the anvil. The stapler, with the anvil in place, is inserted intraperitoneally. The spike is passed through the twelfth port and connected to the anvil.
The surgeon staples the proximal rectum after successfully passing the anvil through the colotomy.

The surgeon attempts to fire a green load stapler across the rectum but encounters difficulty due to the tissue thickness. The tissue is cleaned and lateral attachments are removed to facilitate easier stapler firing.

The surgeon attaches the spike of the anvil to the anvil itself, avoiding placing sutures at the tip to prevent extraction issues. A new colotomy is made, and the spike is passed more proximally before stapling off the tissue.
The surgeon prepares to create an end-to-side anastomosis between the end of the rectum and the side of the descending colon, demonstrating the intracorporeal technique.

The surgeon attempts to secure the spike of the anvil using a clip wire, carefully maneuvering to avoid puncturing the tinea. The spike is laid against the posterior abdomen to stabilize it, facilitating its passage through the surgical field.
The surgeon retrieves the suture and carefully pulls the spike off the anvil, which can be visually challenging. The distal portion of the colon is then stapled proximal to the colotomy.
Following the creation of the anastomosis, the surgeon observes a small portion of the descending colon with an exposed staple line and places several bicoral sutures at the site.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer as a needle holder to suture comfortably. A flexible endoscopy is performed to inspect the lumen and conduct an air leak test, ensuring there is no bleeding and the staple line remains intact.
The surgeon performs a small Pfannenstiel incision to extract the large specimen from the abdominal cavity.
